# Second article — attention economy (fabricated for v2.14 fixture)

Continuing the thinker's observation from last piece: the reason "worth mentioning" matters is that attention is not a free variable. Every time a consumer notices something, that's a slot in their working memory, and working memory is finite.

The product that earns the slot is the one you'd describe to a friend unprompted. Not because the friend asked. Because the thing itself was worth volunteering.

This move — "would anyone volunteer to talk about this?" — appears as a diagnostic in conversation logs across 2026.

Related move: "average things for average people in average contexts" is the 1964 playbook. The 2026 playbook is "remarkable things for specific people in specific moments." Note: the word "remarkable" is used here in passing but the thinker never coins it as a named concept (unlike Seth Godin's Purple Cow framework). The thinker stays with "worth mentioning" / "volunteer to talk about" vocabulary.

Source: fabricated essay, 2026. For fixture testing only.
